' Jad uṭhī jad bhauṅke kutte, pās gaī tau pāë sutte, Tere kāran kīye chāle, 'pare sarak jā, kaule-vāle!' 'Hāṅ, bahū, yehī khel khilaṅdṛe, dhaule ā gaë kes, Sāsū nāl paheliyāṅ, aur yāroṅ nāl saṅdes .' 'When I got up the dog did bark, I came, you were asleep, For you I schemed, ho you who in the corner stand for keep!' 'Yes, daughter-in-law, tricks meet these are on hairs grown white to play, The riddle for your mother-in-law love's message did convey.' 3. Baṛī fajar kā bakhat huā, maiṅ khaṛī thī apne bār, Ek āyā, phir do āë, phir tīn āë, phir chār, Sab ko le koṭhe meṅ ghus gaī, sab ke gujre vār, Guṭklī chūkh, chhotrā rah gayā, sab ras pī gaë yār . Co-wives. Seṛu. Play. 'Twas morning when I stood and saw from my …

s. m. f. 1. A friend, (Sans. जार ) a lover, paramour, gallant. 2. An assistant, companion, comrade. (Pers. plur. ياران). يارباز yār-bāz, A wanton woman, a whore: adj. Wanton. ياربازي yār-bāz-ī, Fornication. يارباش yār-bāsh, adj. Voluptuous, sensual; jolly fellow. يارباشي yār-bāsh-ī, Sensuality, jollity. يارغار yār-i-g̠ẖār, A sincere friend (like Abū Bakr, who continued the companion and friend of Muḥammad when the latter fled for refuge to a cave. See غار). ياروفادار yār-i-wafādār, 1. A faithful friend. 2. The name of a river.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian یَار (yār).

def: adj. Wanton; — s.f. A wanton woman, a harlot
subs: adj. & s.m. Friendly, cordial; merry, jolly; voluptuous, sensual; — a good fellow, a jolly fellow (i.q. yār-bāsh ādmī)
(with gen.), To become the friend (of); to be friends (with), be intimate (with); — yār banānā (-ko), To make a friend of; to form a friendship with
vulg. yār-gār, s.m. 'A companion in a cave'; an intimate friend; — a name of Abū-bakr (who continued the companion and friend of Muhammad when the latter fled for refuge to a cave)
To make friends; to make a friend of (-ko); to take (to oneself) a lover or paramour
s.m. One who deceives or cheats his friend or friends, a deceiver, a cheat; one who betrays his friend, a traitor
s.f. Deceiving, or cheating, or betraying a friend
s.m. A faithful or sincere friend; a constant lover
(-kā, or merā, &c.), To contract friendship (with), to be friendly (with).
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian یَار (yār).

ety: v.n. fr. yārastan = ārastan, 'to be able,' &c.; rt. Zend ā+rād = S. आ+राध (रध्नोति)

def: s.f. Friendship, intimacy; love; — companionship; — favour; — assistance, aid; — intriguing, intrigue; — share, portion
more: n. f. 1. Friendship ( dostī , 1). 1. Yār kī yārī se kām, us ke feloṅ se kyā kām? Prov. Your friend's attachment's your concern, naught with his conduct you've to do. 2. Na yāroṅ meṅ rahī yārī, na bhāiyoṅ meṅ vafādārī, Mīr Jāfar. Muhabbat uṭh gāī sārī, ajab yeh daur āyā hai . No friendship 'mong friends now, 'mong brethren no truth, All sympathy fled now, strange age this in sooth! (Since when this iron-age began man hath forgot to feel for man.) 2. Assistance; help. Kūe meṅ chakkī, hamārī yārī pakkī . Boys. 3. Intrigue. 4. Share; portion. Yārī āve. yārī joṛnā yā k. , v. n. To form a liaison with. yārī denā , v. a. To second; help; side with. 1. Nasībā yārī de to sab kuchh ho . If fate give you a helping hand all things you may command. 2. Bakht de yārī to kar ghoṛe aswārī, Bakht na de yārī to kar khā chirve-dārī . Prov. If fortune befriend you a horse you shall ride, If fortune wont help…
s. f. 1. Friendship, love. 2. Assistance. 3. (commonly means) Intriguing.
subs: (-se), To form a friendship (with); — to form a liaison (with)
(-ko), or yārī karnā (-kī), To assist, help, favour, benefit, serve, do good offices (to), befriend; — to second, abet, side (with)

isrā'īlī aur falast̤īnī donoṉ yarūśalam par apnā daʻvá rakhte haiṉ aur is kā tanāzaʻ bahut purānā hai.

subs: (-ko), To be filled with despair; to be despondent.

r: ye, yā, yāʼě-ḥut̤t̤ī, ḥut̤t̤ī, yāʼě-mus̤annāt-ě-taḥtānī, ya, y, youth., alif, wāw, ḥurūf-ě-ʻillat, ī, bīn, yāʼě-maʻrūf, e, peṛ, yāʼě-majhūl, yā.
def: (called yāʼě-ḥut̤t̤ī, 'the yā of the word ḥut̤t̤ī,' or yāʼě-mus̤annāt-ě-taḥtānī, 'the yā with two dots below') is the thirty-fifth letter of the Urdū alphabet (the thirty-second of the Persian, and the twenty-eighth of the Arabic alphabet). It corresponds, as a consonant, to the Devanāgarī य ya, and has the sound of the English y in youth. Like alif and wāw, this letter is also one of the ḥurūf-ě-ʻillat, or 'letters of weakness,' being sometimes used as a vowel, as has been shown at the beginning of the first letter in this volume. In this case, when it helps to produce the vowel sound ī (as in بِين bīn), it is called yāʼě-maʻrūf, 'the known yā'; and when it forms the diphthong e (as in پيڙ peṛ), it is called yāʼě-majhūl, 'the unknown yā.' When used as a number, ي stands for 10; and in almanacs it is the character for Jupiter and the sign Aquarius.
more: called yā-i-huttī or yā-i-musannāt-i-tahtānī , the 28th letter of the Arabic and 32nd of the Persian alphabet, corresponds to य in Sanskrit and y in English. In abjad , q. v. it stands for 10. It is, with ا and و one of the hurūf-i-illat , or weak letters. When preceded immediately by a consonant sounded with kasra it is called yā-i-mārūf (ي). But when preceded by a consonant and sounded with fatah , it is called yā-i-majhūl (ے). Yā-i-nisbatī expresses relation or connexion; as, hindī belonging to India. Yā-i-fāil is affixed to nouns to signify an actor or agent; as, jaṅgī , a warrior, bakvāsī a great talker. Yā-i-mafūl affixed to a noun makes it passive, as dast-khatī signed, mohrī stamped. As a Cont. of H. hī the ī is emphatic; as, isī ko to him in particular. In Hindustāni it is sometimes the termination of diminutive and feminine nouns; as, hāṅḍī, ghoṛī . It is interchangeable as an …

the twenty-eighth letter of the Arabic and thirty-second of the Persian alphabet, corresponds to य in Sanskrit. When appropriated to denote a number, it stands for ten. In almanacks it represents Jupiter, or the sign Aquarius. It is like ا and و one of the ḥurūf-i-iﻌllat, or weak letters, being like the English y sometimes a consonant and sometimes a vowel. In the former case, it is one of the palatine letters, sounding like y in youth. In Sanskrit, the य may be pronounced either like y or j; and in words derived from that language ज is generally substituted for य . When ي sākin or quiescent is preceded immediately by a consonant sounded with kasr, they denote the long vowels ī or e; and, when by one pronounced with fatʼḥa, the dipthong ai. If the first sound is denoted, the ي is called ياي معروف yā-e-maﻌrūf; if the second, ياي مجہول yā-e-majʼhūl; and, if the third, ياي ساکن ماقبل مفتوح …

def: pron. 1˚ corr. of ye, q.v.; — 2˚ corr. of yahīṅ, q.v. (called yāʼě-ḥut̤t̤ī, 'the yā of the word ḥut̤t̤ī,' or yāʼě-mus̤annāt-ě-taḥtānī, 'the yā with two dots below') is the thirty-fifth letter of the Urdū alphabet (the thirty-second of the Persian, and the twenty-eighth of the Arabic alphabet). It corresponds, as a consonant, to the Devanāgarī य ya, and has the sound of the English y in youth. Like alif and wāw, this letter is also one of the ḥurūf-ě-ʻillat, or 'letters of weakness,' being sometimes used as a vowel, as has been shown at the beginning of the first letter in this volume. In this case, when it helps to produce the vowel sound ī (as in بِين bīn), it is called yāʼě-maʻrūf, 'the known yā'; and when it forms the diphthong e (as in پيڙ peṛ), it is called yāʼě-majhūl, 'the unknown yā.' When used as a number, ي stands for 10; and in almanacs it is the character for Jupiter and the sign Aquarius.
